allopack.title: Main Idea — What Is This Really About?
allopack.author: AlloFlow community catalog (authored by Claude, reviewed by A. Pomeranz)
allopack.standards: CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.4.2 (determine the main idea of a text and explain how it is supported by key details); CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.5.2 (determine two or more main ideas and explain how they are supported by key details); CCSS.ELA-LITERACY.RI.4.1 (refer to details and examples when explaining what a text says)

history.title: Find What It Is Really About

data.body:
**Due:** (your teacher will tell you)

Here is a skill that shows up on every test you will ever take, and in every article you will ever read: figuring out what a text is *really* about.

1. Read **The Suitcase Test** — it gives you a trick you can use forever.
2. Learn the words in the **Glossary**, then win the **Word Scramble**.
3. Keep the **Main Idea Moves** chart open while you work.
4. Practice on **Which Detail Does Not Belong?**
5. Take the quiz — it uses short paragraphs, so read each one twice.
6. Use the **Sentence Frames** to write your own main-idea statements.

Watch out for the most common trap: the topic is not the main idea. "Dogs" is a topic. "Dogs can be trained to detect illness" is a main idea. 🧳

objectives.label: I found the main idea of something I read outside of school

history.title: The Suitcase Test
history.data:
## The Suitcase Test

Imagine you are packing a suitcase for a trip. The suitcase is the **main idea** — the one big container. The clothes you pack inside are the **supporting details**. Everything that goes in has to belong to that trip.

When you read a paragraph, you are doing the same job in reverse. You look at what is packed and figure out what suitcase it all came from.

Here is a paragraph:

> Honeybees do far more than make honey. As they move from flower to flower, pollen sticks to their bodies and travels with them, which lets plants make seeds. About one out of every three bites of food people eat depends on a pollinator like a bee. Farmers of almonds, apples, and blueberries even rent beehives during flowering season.

What is it about? A first instinct is "bees" — but that is only the **topic**, the one-word subject. The main idea is a full thought: *honeybees are important to our food supply because they pollinate crops.*

That is the difference students miss most. The topic is a word. The main idea is a sentence that says something ABOUT the topic.

Notice how the details worked. Pollen sticking to bees, one in three bites, farmers renting hives — each one supports the same claim. That is your test: if a sentence does not help prove the main idea, it does not belong in that suitcase.

Sometimes the main idea is stated right in the text, often in the first or last sentence. That is the easy version. Other times it is **implied** — never written down anywhere, and you have to build it yourself from the details. Both count. Both are findable.

A quick method that works: read the whole paragraph, then cover it and ask yourself, "If I could only say one sentence about this, what would it be?" Whatever you say out loud is usually very close to the main idea.

One warning. A detail can be true, interesting, and still not be the main idea. "Bees have five eyes" is a fact, and it is fascinating, but a paragraph about pollination is not really about bee eyes. Interesting and important are different things — and telling them apart is most of what good reading is.

data.def: The most important point a text makes — a complete thought, not one word.
What a text is about in a word or short phrase.
A smaller piece of information that supports the main idea.
To help prove or explain a bigger point.
A short retelling of the most important parts.
Written directly in the text where you can point to it.
Suggested but not written down — you build it from clues.
The specific words or facts from a text that back up an answer.
A group of sentences that work together on one idea.
Actually related to the point being made.

sections.label: TOPIC vs IDEA
bullets: Main idea = a SENTENCE about it
If you can say it in one word, keep going
sections.iconPrompt: a single word tag beside a full sentence

sections.label: LOOK FIRST AND LAST
bullets: Often stated in sentence 1 or the last one
But not always — sometimes it is implied
Implied still counts and is still findable
sections.iconPrompt: a paragraph with the first and last lines highlighted

sections.label: THE COVER TEST
bullets: Read it, cover it, say one sentence
What you say out loud is usually close
Then check it against the details

sections.label: CHECK THE DETAILS
bullets: Every detail should support the idea
If one does not fit, your idea may be too narrow
Interesting is NOT the same as important
sections.iconPrompt: three suitcase items with checkmarks and one with an x

history.title: Which Detail Does Not Belong?

categories.label: Supports: 'Bees matter to our food supply'
True, but does NOT support that idea

items.content: Pollen sticks to bees and travels between flowers
About one in three bites of our food depends on pollinators
Almond farmers rent beehives during flowering season
A bee's wings beat over 200 times per second
Without pollination, many crops would produce far less fruit
Honey has been found in ancient tombs, still edible
Blueberry yields drop sharply when bee populations fall

history.meta: 2 buckets • 8 details • all TRUE, only some relevant

history.title: Find the Big Point

questions.question: What is the difference between a topic and a main idea?
options: A topic is a word or phrase; a main idea is a full sentence about that topic
A topic is longer than a main idea
A topic is always in the first sentence
questions.correctAnswer: A topic is a word or phrase; a main idea is a full sentence about that topic

questions.question: Read: 'Sea otters eat sea urchins. Urchins eat kelp. Where otters were hunted out, urchins multiplied and kelp forests vanished. Where otters returned, kelp grew back.' What is the main idea?
options: Sea otters protect kelp forests by keeping urchin numbers down
Kelp forests are found in the ocean
questions.correctAnswer: Sea otters protect kelp forests by keeping urchin numbers down

questions.question: A main idea that is never written in the text is called what?
You picked a main idea, but one detail in the paragraph does not support it. What does that most likely mean?
options: Your main idea may be too narrow — reconsider it
That detail should be ignored completely
The paragraph has two topics and cannot be summarized
questions.correctAnswer: Your main idea may be too narrow — reconsider it

questions.question: Which of these is a MAIN IDEA rather than a topic?
options: Volcanoes can create new land over time
questions.correctAnswer: Volcanoes can create new land over time

questions.question: Read: 'Some hospitals now keep therapy dogs on staff. Patients who spend time with them report less pain and lower stress. Nurses say children agree to difficult procedures more easily when a dog is present.' Write the main idea in one sentence.
questions.expectedAnswer: Therapy dogs help patients in hospitals by reducing pain and stress and making difficult procedures easier — especially for children.

questions.question: Explain the difference between a detail being INTERESTING and a detail being IMPORTANT to the main idea. Use an example.
questions.expectedAnswer: An interesting detail catches attention but may not help prove the point. For example, 'bees have five eyes' is interesting, but in a paragraph about bees and our food supply it does not support the main idea, so it is not important there.

reflections.text: What is the main idea of the last thing you read outside of school?
Which is harder for you: finding a stated main idea or an implied one? Why?

history.meta: 5 MCQ + 2 short answer • real paragraphs to analyze

items.text: The topic of this text is ____.
One detail that supports this is ____.
Another detail that supports this is ____.
The detail about ____ is interesting, but it does not support the main idea because ____.
I think the main idea is stated / implied because ____.

data.rubric:
| Criteria | 1 — Starting | 3 — Getting There | 5 — Strong Reader |
|---|---|---|---|
| Main idea | Gives a topic word | A sentence, but too narrow or too broad | A sentence that covers the whole text exactly |
| Evidence | No details cited | One supporting detail | Two or more details quoted or paraphrased accurately |
| Judgment | Treats all details equally | Notices one off-topic detail | Explains WHY a detail does or does not support the idea |

data.question: Can a paragraph have more than one main idea? 🤔
data.answer: A single paragraph usually has one. Whole articles often have several, one per section — which is exactly what the 5th grade standard asks you to handle.

data.question: Is the first sentence always the main idea?
data.answer: Often, but definitely not always. Writers sometimes build up to it and put it last, and sometimes never state it at all. Check the details before trusting the first line.

data.question: What is the difference between a main idea and a summary? 📝
data.answer: The main idea is the single biggest point. A summary is short but includes the main idea PLUS the most important supporting details.

data.question: Why do tests love this skill so much?
data.answer: Because it is the skill underneath almost every other reading skill. If you can find what a text is really about, you can answer most questions about it.

data.question: What if I disagree with the author's main idea?
data.answer: Totally allowed — and identifying it accurately is what makes disagreeing possible. You cannot argue with a point you have not correctly understood first.

data.question: Does this work on videos and podcasts too? 🎧
data.answer: Exactly the same. Ask what one sentence captures the whole thing. The suitcase test does not care what format the ideas arrive in.
